diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute behavioural crisis / self-injurious behaviour in ASD
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Head-banging is a hallmark self-injurious behaviour precipitated by distress/routine disruption.
New or intensified self-injury directed at the head/body can localise or signal pain in a non-verbal patient.

symptoms.symptom_name: Physical aggression and biting toward caregivers

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Aggression endangering staff defines an uncontained behavioural emergency requiring police-assisted response.

symptoms.symptom_name: Acute onset after an unexpected change to the evening routine

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute behavioural crisis / self-injurious behaviour in ASD
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Routine disruption is a recognised environmental trigger for behavioural escalation in autism.
A plausible trigger does not exclude a coincident medical cause; a trigger and a hidden driver can co-exist.

symptoms.symptom_name: Profound communication impairment (non-verbal, cannot report pain)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Inability to self-report pain is precisely why an occult medical/pain cause must be actively excluded rather than assumed away.

symptoms.symptom_name: Frontal scalp hematoma (4x4cm boggy swelling, fresh contusions) from head-banging

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A boggy scalp hematoma indicates significant force and mandates head-injury assessment and neuro-observation.

symptoms.symptom_name: Tachycardia, hypertension and tachypnoea (HR 115, BP 140/90, RR 24)

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Occult acute painful medical driver / physiological distress
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Sympathetic activation from pain, distress or an underlying illness; measured clinician vitals, sealed and not obtainable via chat.

symptoms.symptom_name: No fever (Temp 36.8), no ear-tugging, normal voiding pattern observed, no visible facial/jaw swelling

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute otitis media / UTI / dental abscess as pain driver
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Lower the probability of these mimics but, on limited distance assessment in a non-verbal patient, do NOT exclude them.

symptoms.symptom_name: Possible reduced bowel movements / occult constipation or urinary retention

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Constipation and urinary retention are common, easily-missed painful drivers of behaviour in this population; surfaced only on targeted questioning.

symptoms.symptom_name: Alert with no vomiting and no pupil asymmetry at present

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Reassuring now but does not exclude evolving injury; must be re-checked, especially once any sedation is given.

symptom_clusters.cluster_name: Non-verbal patient with acute behavioural change and possible occult pain
symptom_clusters.target_diagnosis: Occult acute painful medical driver (diagnostic overshadowing screen)
symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: The combination of a non-verbal patient, a new severe behavioural crisis and any hint of a physiological change should trigger an active medical/pain search rather than attribution to the disability.
Ongoing head impacts plus an established hematoma define the physical-emergency limb requiring head-injury assessment and neuro-observation.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: In-person head-injury assessment with neurological observations (pupils, GCS, focal signs)
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Repeated forceful head-banging with a boggy frontal hematoma requires urgent assessment for intracranial injury; cannot be performed via chat.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Systematic pain / medical-cause screen (abdominal exam and bowel/bladder assessment, dental and oral exam, otoscopy, temperature)
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: A non-verbal patient's behaviour may be the only sign of a treatable painful condition; screen for constipation, urinary retention, dental abscess, otitis media and other sources.
Simple, high-yield test to exclude a UTI as a painful behavioural driver in a patient who cannot report urinary symptoms.
Indicated if focal neurology, persistent vomiting, reducing consciousness, or ongoing high-force impacts — decided in person, not reflexively ordered.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Routine bloods / metabolic screen as directed in person
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Consider for an occult medical driver of acute behavioural change once the patient is safe; not a telehealth action.
